method3.generated_article:
==Volcano and caldera==
Hunga Tonga–Hunga Haʻapai is a largely submarine volcano on the Tonga segment of the Tonga–Kermadec intra-oceanic arc in the South Pacific Ocean.[1] The arc is produced by subduction of the Pacific Plate beneath the Indo-Australian Plate and forms part of a subduction system extending about 2,550 km between New Zealand and Tonga.[1] The volcanic edifice rises more than 1.8 km from the seafloor and is about 20 km across, with only small portions historically emergent above sea level.[1] Its summit contains a large caldera, described as about 5 km in diameter, whose rim formed the uninhabited islands of Hunga Tonga to the north and Hunga Haʻapai to the west.[1][5]

Geological observations before the 2022 eruption supported the interpretation that the two islands were remnants of a submarine caldera rim.[5] Bryan et al. described the islands as elongated features tangent to a circle centered on a rocky shoal about 3 km south of Hunga Tonga, near the sites of recorded eruptions in 1912 and 1937.[5] Samples from Hunga Tonga showed alternating layers of andesitic lava flows, scoria, lapilli and ash dipping gently away from the inferred caldera center.[5] High-resolution multibeam sonar surveys later identified a 4 × 2 km, 150 m deep depression at the expected caldera location, consistent with collapse during an earlier explosive eruption.[5] The volcano’s eruptive products are andesitic, a composition common in intra-oceanic arc volcanoes.[1]

Eruptions in 2014–2015 built a large tephra cone between Hunga Tonga and Hunga Haʻapai, joining the two islands into a single landmass named Hunga Tonga–Hunga Haʻapai.[1][5] The new island attracted scientific interest because erosion and landform change there could be monitored in detail and compared with similar landforms on other planets, including Mars.[1] By late 2021 the combined island still broadly resembled its post-2015 form despite shoreline erosion and settling.[5] Explosive activity resumed in December 2021 and January 2022, culminating in the 15 January 2022 eruption.[5][3] After that eruption, the 2014–2015 cone and most of the combined island were destroyed, leaving only small remnants of Hunga Tonga and Hunga Haʻapai above the ocean.[1]

==Geography==
The volcano is in the South Pacific Ocean near the island nation of Tonga.[3][4] Its setting is remote within the wider Pacific: shock and pressure waves from the 2022 eruption were reported in Fiji about 500 km to the northwest, in New Zealand about 1,600–2,000 km away, and in Alaska roughly 9,370 km to the northeast.[1] The January 2022 eruption occurred in a shallow marine setting, with the active vent reported to be about 200 m below the ocean surface.[4] This shallow-water geography helped couple the eruption to both the ocean and atmosphere, producing tsunamigenic effects that researchers have associated with atmospheric–oceanographic resonance.[5] Along Tonga’s coasts, the resulting tsunami reached nearly 15 m in places and destroyed villages, making the eruption a major coastal-geomorphic event as well as a volcanic one.[3] The site’s small and rapidly changing landforms have been documented extensively by satellite imagery, including NASA observations before and after the January 2022 eruption.[1] Scientists have emphasized the need to study recent tsunami deposits in Tonga before they are altered by human activity, tropical storms, or other coastal processes.[5]

==Eruptions==
The climactic eruption on 15 January 2022 was described as the largest underwater explosion recorded by modern scientific instruments and among the largest volcanic events observed in recent decades.[1][2] It lofted volcanic material to about 58 km above Earth’s surface, placing the plume well into the middle atmosphere.[2] The blast generated a pressure wave and sonic boom that travelled around the world, with reports that the atmospheric wave circled the globe twice.[2] The eruptive plume also produced exceptional lightning activity for several hours, with more lightning over Tonga and nearby waters than the rest of the world combined during that period.[5]

The eruption coupled energy into the solid Earth, ocean and atmosphere, producing waves that were recorded globally.[5] Modelling and wave measurements indicated that the event released about an order of magnitude more energy than the 1980 eruption of Mount St. Helens.[5] Researchers have described the eruption as a rare “natural laboratory” for studying underwater volcanic explosions, shock waves and tsunami generation.[5]

A distinctive feature of the eruption was the exceptionally large injection of water vapour into the stratosphere, increasing global stratospheric water vapour by about 10%.[1][5] The eruption also emitted sulfur dioxide, which was converted into sulfate aerosols that reflect sunlight and tend to cool the atmosphere.[3] Because water vapour and sulfate aerosols have opposing radiative effects, the eruption prompted extensive study of its possible climate impacts.[3]

Subsequent analyses found that the eruption disturbed Southern Hemisphere stratospheric ozone in the months after the event, but had only minor overall effects on the Antarctic ozone hole and surface climate.[1] Model simulations indicated a global surface cooling influence of about 0.05 K, small enough to be indistinguishable from natural climate variability.[1] A UCLA-led study reported a cooling of about 0.1 °C over the Southern Hemisphere, with only limited indications of a slight warming influence in the Northern Hemisphere from persistent stratospheric water vapour.[3] Scientific assessments concluded that the eruption did not cause the record-high global temperatures observed in 2023–2024.[1]

The eruption stimulated international collaboration combining satellite observations, field evidence, data analysis and climate modelling.[1][4] The Hunga Tonga–Hunga Haʻapai Impact Model Observation Comparison project brought together more than ten global climate models to compare simulations of the eruption’s atmospheric effects.[1] Studies of the event have also emphasized its importance for understanding historical and prehistoric Pacific tsunamis, as well as the role of coupled atmospheric–oceanographic resonance in tsunami behaviour.[4]

==Footnotes==
* The volcano is an underwater edifice in the Tonga–Kermadec volcanic arc at about 20.536°S, 175.382°W, with its vent described before the 2022 eruption as roughly 150–200 m below sea level.[1][4]
* Its 15 January 2022 eruption has been described as one of the largest volcanic events recorded with modern instruments and the largest underwater explosion so recorded.[1][2]
* The eruption column reached the upper atmosphere, with estimates of about 57–58 km altitude.[1][3]
* The event generated a tsunami reported at nearly 15 m in Tonga, destroyed villages, and produced a sonic boom that propagated around the world twice.[3]
* The eruption expelled approximately 2.9 × 10¹⁵ g of basaltic-andesitic volcanogenic material, including ash and pumice, into the South Pacific Gyre.[1]
* Satellite observations after ash deposition recorded elevated surface chlorophyll-a in nearby ocean waters.[1]
* The eruption injected unusually large amounts of water vapour into the stratosphere, increasing global stratospheric water by about 10% and leaving a substantial fraction present through 2025.[2]
* Studies cited in the documents indicate that the eruption’s overall effect on surface climate was small, with modelled global cooling of about 0.05 K and Southern Hemisphere cooling estimated at about 0.1 °C in one UCLA-led study.[2][4]
* Scientific assessments concluded that the eruption did not cause the record-high global temperatures observed in 2023–2024.[2]
* The eruption has prompted renewed scientific study of tsunami generation, atmospheric–oceanic resonance, tsunami deposits, and the stratigraphy of South Pacific volcanoes.[5]

Hunga Tonga–Hunga Haʻapai is a largely submarine volcano on the Tonga segment of the Tonga–Kermadec intra-oceanic arc in the South Pacific Ocean.[1] The arc is produced by subduction of the Pacific Plate beneath the Indo-Australian Plate and forms part of a subduction system extending about 2,550 km between New Zealand and Tonga.[1] The volcanic edifice rises more than 1.8 km from the seafloor and is about 20 km across, with only small portions historically emergent above sea level.[1] Its summit contains a large caldera, described as about 5 km in diameter, whose rim formed the uninhabited islands of Hunga Tonga to the north and Hunga Haʻapai to the west.[1][5]

The volcano is in the South Pacific Ocean near the island nation of Tonga.[3][4] Its setting is remote within the wider Pacific: shock and pressure waves from the 2022 eruption were reported in Fiji about 500 km to the northwest, in New Zealand about 1,600–2,000 km away, and in Alaska roughly 9,370 km to the northeast.[1] The January 2022 eruption occurred in a shallow marine setting, with the active vent reported to be about 200 m below the ocean surface.[4] This shallow-water geography helped couple the eruption to both the ocean and atmosphere, producing tsunamigenic effects that researchers have associated with atmospheric–oceanographic resonance.[5] Along Tonga’s coasts, the resulting tsunami reached nearly 15 m in places and destroyed villages, making the eruption a major coastal-geomorphic event as well as a volcanic one.[3] The site’s small and rapidly changing landforms have been documented extensively by satellite imagery, including NASA observations before and after the January 2022 eruption.[1] Scientists have emphasized the need to study recent tsunami deposits in Tonga before they are altered by human activity, tropical storms, or other coastal processes.[5]

The climactic eruption on 15 January 2022 was described as the largest underwater explosion recorded by modern scientific instruments and among the largest volcanic events observed in recent decades.[1][2] It lofted volcanic material to about 58 km above Earth’s surface, placing the plume well into the middle atmosphere.[2] The blast generated a pressure wave and sonic boom that travelled around the world, with reports that the atmospheric wave circled the globe twice.[2] The eruptive plume also produced exceptional lightning activity for several hours, with more lightning over Tonga and nearby waters than the rest of the world combined during that period.[5]

The eruption coupled energy into the solid Earth, ocean and atmosphere, producing waves that were recorded globally.[5] Modelling and wave measurements indicated that the event released about an order of magnitude more energy than the 1980 eruption of Mount St. Helens.[5] Researchers have described the eruption as a rare “natural laboratory” for studying underwater volcanic explosions, shock waves and tsunami generation.[5]

A distinctive feature of the eruption was the exceptionally large injection of water vapour into the stratosphere, increasing global stratospheric water vapour by about 10%.[1][5] The eruption also emitted sulfur dioxide, which was converted into sulfate aerosols that reflect sunlight and tend to cool the atmosphere.[3] Because water vapour and sulfate aerosols have opposing radiative effects, the eruption prompted extensive study of its possible climate impacts.[3]

Subsequent analyses found that the eruption disturbed Southern Hemisphere stratospheric ozone in the months after the event, but had only minor overall effects on the Antarctic ozone hole and surface climate.[1] Model simulations indicated a global surface cooling influence of about 0.05 K, small enough to be indistinguishable from natural climate variability.[1] A UCLA-led study reported a cooling of about 0.1 °C over the Southern Hemisphere, with only limited indications of a slight warming influence in the Northern Hemisphere from persistent stratospheric water vapour.[3] Scientific assessments concluded that the eruption did not cause the record-high global temperatures observed in 2023–2024.[1]

The eruption stimulated international collaboration combining satellite observations, field evidence, data analysis and climate modelling.[1][4] The Hunga Tonga–Hunga Haʻapai Impact Model Observation Comparison project brought together more than ten global climate models to compare simulations of the eruption’s atmospheric effects.[1] Studies of the event have also emphasized its importance for understanding historical and prehistoric Pacific tsunamis, as well as the role of coupled atmospheric–oceanographic resonance in tsunami behaviour.[4]

* The volcano is an underwater edifice in the Tonga–Kermadec volcanic arc at about 20.536°S, 175.382°W, with its vent described before the 2022 eruption as roughly 150–200 m below sea level.[1][4]
* Its 15 January 2022 eruption has been described as one of the largest volcanic events recorded with modern instruments and the largest underwater explosion so recorded.[1][2]
* The eruption column reached the upper atmosphere, with estimates of about 57–58 km altitude.[1][3]
* The event generated a tsunami reported at nearly 15 m in Tonga, destroyed villages, and produced a sonic boom that propagated around the world twice.[3]
* The eruption expelled approximately 2.9 × 10¹⁵ g of basaltic-andesitic volcanogenic material, including ash and pumice, into the South Pacific Gyre.[1]
* Satellite observations after ash deposition recorded elevated surface chlorophyll-a in nearby ocean waters.[1]
* The eruption injected unusually large amounts of water vapour into the stratosphere, increasing global stratospheric water by about 10% and leaving a substantial fraction present through 2025.[2]
* Studies cited in the documents indicate that the eruption’s overall effect on surface climate was small, with modelled global cooling of about 0.05 K and Southern Hemisphere cooling estimated at about 0.1 °C in one UCLA-led study.[2][4]
* Scientific assessments concluded that the eruption did not cause the record-high global temperatures observed in 2023–2024.[2]
* The eruption has prompted renewed scientific study of tsunami generation, atmospheric–oceanic resonance, tsunami deposits, and the stratigraphy of South Pacific volcanoes.[5]
